coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from Calotropis procera leaves for trace level identification of isoprothiolane
coredata.dc:creator: Ghosh, Subhadeep
Gul, Anam Rana
Park, Chan Yeong
Xu, Ping
Baek, Seung Hoon
Bhamore, Jigna R.
Kim, Min Woo
Lee, Min
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
Park, Tae Jung
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanocrystals play a crucial role in developing drug delivery and bioimaging systems because of their biocompatible nature, unique surface functionalities, and optical characteristics. In this study, we focused on the one-pot simple and convenient synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from Calotropis procera leaves through hydrothermal carbonization. The synthesized CDs exhibit tremendous excitation-dependent emission spectra with a high quantum yield of 71.95% and do not require any surface modifications to enhance their fluorescence. The CDs show a robust emission peak at 416 nm. The exact size and stability (long-lasting in aqueous solution) of the fluorescent probe were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, UV–vis absorption, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. We obtained results indicating that the emission peak of CDs was significantly decreased with the addition of isoprothiolane. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the probe was not hampered vastly under different pH conditions. The detection limit for isoprothiolane was found to be 11.58 nM (3.36 ppb), and the CD-based fluorescence approach was applied to detect isoprothiolane pesticide in fruit and rice samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Calotropis procera leaves
Fluorescence
Isoprothiolane
Spectroscopic techniques

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of isoprothiolane recovery from spiked samples
Analysis of isoprothiolane in rice and fruit samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT ( NRF-2019R1A2C2084065 ) and by the Chung-Ang University Research Grants in 2019.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra (λex: 340 nm, λem: 416 nm) and UV–vis spectra of CDs from C. procera leaves through hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 4 h (Inset image of CDs at daylight and UV lamp condition at 365 nm).

XPS spectra of CDs. (a) Survey spectrum, (b) High-resolution spectra of C1s, (c) High-resolution spectra of O1s, (d) High-resolution spectra of N1s, (e) XRD spectra of CDs derived from dried C. procera leaves.
FE-TEM images of CDs at (a) 10 nm and (b) 5 nm scale bars. (c) Histogram data.

Fluorescence intensities of CDs with various pesticides (sulfosulfuron, fipronil, clodinafop, imidacloprid, isoproturon, carbendazim, thiamethoxam, hexaconazole, tricyclazole, and isoprothiolane at 1 mM (λex: 340 nm, λem: 416 nm).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs with the addition of different concentrations of isoprothiolane ranging with 0.05–1,000 µM (λex: 340 nm, λem: 416 nm). (b) Calibration curve constructed between Io/I and different concentrations of isoprothiolane (0.05–100 µM).
Hydrolysis of isoprothiolane and its binding mechanism with CDs.
Synthesis of CDs from Calotropis procera leaves via the hydrothermal method and their applications in detecting isoprothiolane pesticide.
Quantum yield obtained from slope method.
XPS elemental composition and content of the CDs.
Performance of the present probe in comparison with other reported analytical methods for the detection of isoprothiolane pesticide.
Fluorescence-lifetime measurement for CDs and CDs with isoprothiolane.
Determination of isoprothiolane in rice and fruit samples using CDs as a fluorometric probe (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from Calotropis procera leaves for trace level identification of isoprothiolane

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Research Institute of Chem-Bio Diagnostic Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Advanced Prosthodontics, Weintraub Center for Reconstructive Biotechnology, University of California, Los Angeles, 10833 Le Conte Avenue, CHS 23-088F, Los Angeles, CA 90095-1668, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Advanced Prosthodontics, Weintraub Center for Reconstructive Biotechnology, University of California, Los Angeles, 10833 Le Conte Avenue, CHS 23-088F, Los Angeles, CA 90095-1668, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanocrystals play a crucial role in developing drug delivery and bioimaging systems because of their biocompatible nature, unique surface functionalities, and optical characteristics. In this study, we focused on the one-pot simple and convenient synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from Calotropis procera leaves through hydrothermal carbonization. The synthesized CDs exhibit tremendous excitation-dependent emission spectra with a high quantum yield of 71.95% and do not require any surface modifications to enhance their fluorescence. The CDs show a robust emission peak at 416 nm. The exact size and stability (long-lasting in aqueous solution) of the fluorescent probe were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, UV–vis absorption, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. We obtained results indicating that the emission peak of CDs was significantly decreased with the addition of isoprothiolane. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the probe was not hampered vastly under different pH conditions. The detection limit for isoprothiolane was found to be 11.58 nM (3.36 ppb), and the CD-based fluorescence approach was applied to detect isoprothiolane pesticide in fruit and rice samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides have been used extensively in order to increase agricultural yield by protecting crops from severe damage that can be caused by pests [1]. It has also drawn considerable attention owing to the health risks they pose to humans, even when used at low concentrations [2]. Nearly one-third of the global production of healthy agricultural products is dependent on the utilization of pesticides. It has been recorded that almost 50% of all harmful agents instigating poisoning all over the world are pesticides [3,4]. It has also been reported that, when spraying pesticides, only about 1% of the pesticide reaches the target crop with the rest rolling out to water and soil, posing risks to human health [1,5]. Isoprothiolane is one of the systematic fungicides of the malonate ester which is used to control numerous crop diseases such as rice blasts, rice stem rot, Helminthosporium sigmoideum, Fusarium nivale, and Pyricularia oryzae [6]. At high levels, isoprothiolane has been reported to cause nervousness among distributors [7]. Before the European commission fixed a limit of 0.01 ppm, it had been previously reported that Indian basmati rice contained up to 0.03% of isoprothiolane [8]. Isoprothiolane has also been reported to have carcinogenic effects on humans when they are exposed to specific concentrations for long periods [9]. To address health risks (such as prolonged disease, neurological maladies, and short-term health issues) linked to isoprothiolane and the various concerns raised about its use, appropriate measures have to be developed in order to control its distribution and use [10]. Several potentiometric standard methods that can detect isoprothiolane have been developed such as gas chromatography (GC) [11], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12], and flow injection colorimetry [13]. Other methods that can detect isoprothiolane have been developed such as immunosorbent assay [8], GC-ECD [14], HPLC–MS/MS [15], GC–MS [16], NPD [17], and LC–MS [18]. These methods have their advantages as well as some shortcomings, including time-consuming sample preparation, high diagnostic costs, the need for advanced equipment, and real-time detection limitations [19]. As such, there is still a need for a swift, selective, sensitive, and simple technique for the detection of pesticides.

Diversification of nanotechnology has led to the discovery of different advanced materials for type-specific applications such as optical sensors based on diagnostic probes. When any nanomaterials show remarkably unique properties from their source or bulk materials, they draw a lot of attention, even more, when they exhibit similar chemical properties to their source materials [20]. Nanomaterials offer several advantages with a high surface area to volume ratio, which precedes a major change like interference chemistry; they also bind with molecules and interact more easily on the surface of active sites [21]. In our case, nanotechnology helped to flourish the luminescent properties of carbon nanocrystals owing to the extremely small radii attained that were smaller than the Bohr exciton radius [22]. Several types of nanomaterials (metal, silica, polymeric, dendritic, liposomal-based nanoparticles, nanocrystals, nanotubes, nanoclusters nanospheres, and nanorods) exhibit the aforementioned properties. Particularly, carbon-based nanoparticles, broadly known as carbon dots (CDs) have fascinated researchers owing to their extraordinary properties such as the zero dimensional (0 D) with sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in its arrangement [23], the presence of an active organic group, and surface passiveness, and biocompatibility [24]. The CDs are used for various applications such as sensing [25–32], drug delivery [33], imaging [34–38], photodynamic agents [39], and catalytic degradation [40]. They can now be synthesized from green sources i.e., leaves and vegetables such as Water hyacinth [41], Green tea leaves [42], Bamboo leaves [43], Azadirachta indica [44]. Mint leaves [45], Tulsi leaves [46], Coriander leaves [47], Saccharum officinarum [48], Solanum tuberosum [49], Solanum lycopersicum [50], Gum tragacanth [51], and carrot [52]. Moreover, they can provide a carbon source for the detection of metal ions and pesticides. Through various methods, detection tests have been successfully carried out on pesticides such as terbuthylazine, dimethoate [53], chlorpyrifos [54], hexachlorocyclohexane [55], pencycuron [56], carbaryl [57], sinapine [58], paraoxon, glyphosate [59], spermine [60], diazinon [61], fenitrothinon [62], pendimethalin [63], diafenthiuron [64], atrazine [65], deltamethrin [66], and many others. Furthermore, our work demonstrates the protocols for detecting isoprothiolane in fruit and rice samples via tests utilizing carbon-based nanomaterials.

In our study, we fabricated green CDs from Calotropis procera leaves by a one-pot hydrothermal procedure for the detection of isoprothiolane pesticides. C. procera, which is obliged to survive in a variety of soil types without taking proper precautions, it is more likely to be drought resilient, salt lenient [67,68]. Dry leaf-water extract of C. procera acts as an allelopathic effect particularly for the seed germination of various crops and fruits [69]. Leaves of C. procera are used as an antidote for mumps, burn injuries, and rheumatism. Moreover, its leaves are also used as fertilizer [70,71]. One of the important parts of C. procera is its latex, which is useful for the treatment of hair fall, tooth discomforts, sporadic fevers, rheumatoid/joints swelling, and paralysis [72]. Medicinal benefits of C. procera leaves make it an exception for its use wide range. Thus, the dried leaves, which are usually used for seed germination and allelopathic effect, were used as a precursor for the synthesis of CDs. Considering the above health benefits and other usefulness of the precursor, we have used this leaf to produce highly stable CDs with great sensitivity and rapid detection of the pesticide. Moreover, intensive blue fluorescence of CDs was observed under the excitation of UV lamp at 365 nm. The CDs showed a strong allure toward the isoprothiolane, which allowed us to establish a detection system via fluorescence turn-off mechanism. In addition, binding between CDs and isoprothiolane was confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy, and further confirmation was done through the decrease in the fluorescence lifetime efficiency. This method exhibited a wide linear range concentration (1.0 mM–0.05 µM) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 11.58 nM (3.36 ppb). Higher sensitivity of CDs towards isoprothiolane makes it possible to achieve an increased detection limit. Furthermore, this method exhibits an evanescent detection time with simplicity in the sample preparation. Moreover, the synthesized CDs shows high sensitivity towards isoprothiolane without any further modifications and above all interference study provided us with the details about its selectivity towards detected pesticide. Finally, the probe was successfully applied for the detection of isoprothiolane in spiked samples of fruit and rice with decent recovery response.

(i) C. procera leaves (accumulated from the SVNIT campus, Surat, India) (ii) Analytical class chemicals without any additional purification of (iii) Mg(NO3)3·6H2O, (iv) MnSO4, (v) Cu(SO4)·5H2O, (vi) NaCl, (vii) KCl, (viii) KBr, (ix) NiSO4·6H2O, (x) Pb(NO3)2, (xi) Fe(OH)3, (xii) FeCl3, (xiii) Co(NO3)·6H2O, (xiv) K2Cr2O7, and (xv) Hg(NO3)2·H2O. Materials ii to xv were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Other materials were (xvi) Isoproturon (Gharda Chemical, Mumbai, India), (xvii) Sulfosulfuron, (xviii) isoprothiolane, (xix) hexaconazole, (xx) carbendazim, (xxi) clodinafop, and (xxii) fipronil. Materials xvi to xxii were received from Super Crop Safe (Ahmedabad, India). (xxiii) Tricyclazole and (xxiv) imidacloprid were received from Atul (Ankleshwar, India). (xxv) Thiamethoxam was received from United Phosphorous (Mumbai, India).

The blue-light-emitting CDs were synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal procedure. To investigate the synthesis, C. procera leaves were collected from the SVNIT campus (Surat, India) and then washed with deionized (DI) water to remove dust particles. Thereafter the leaves were ground using a grinder, and ground leaves were let to completely dry in direct sunlight for 2 days. The ground and dried leaves were then weighed and mixed with DI water at the ratio of 1:10. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 4 h for complete carbonization. The reacted mixture was then filtered using a Whatman filter paper (Grade 292; 125 mm in diameter) to remove the larger particles, and the solution was further centrifuged against 10,062 × g to remove any further larger particles and finally dialyzed (Dialysis membrane 70; 17.5 mm in diameter) at a ratio of 1:2 for 9 h to obtain equally sized CDs. Finally, the dialyzed solution was stored at 4 °C for further use. A schematic diagram is shown in Scheme 1 .

In a characteristic experiment, fluorescence detection of isoprothiolane was performed out as follows: 1 mL solution of the CDs was put into a 4 mL glass vial. Subsequently, 0.5 mL 1.0 mM solution of isoprothiolane was added to the glass vial followed separately by vortex after 45 s. The mixture was kept for 5 min for fluorescence spectra measurement (λex at 340 nm, λem at 416 nm). A calibration graph was constructed for fluorescence variations intensity against isoprothiolane concentrations. To explore selectivity, some frequent interfering pesticides were selected. The selected pesticides were isoproturon, thiamethoxam, sulfosulfuron, tricyclazole, imidacloprid, and fipronil. All of them were tested in similar conditions.

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs was measured using a guideline reference sample (quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4) with 71.95% QY and an emission wavelength of 416 nm. The standard equation is as follows:
QCDs = QR *(ICDs/IR) * (AR/ACDs) * (η2 CDs/η2 R),
where “Q” is the QY of the CDs, “QR” is the QY of quinine sulfate, the CDs pointed in the subscript are carbon nanocrystals, “R” mentioned in the subscript is indicated as a reference, “I” is the measured integrated emission intensity, “A” is UV-absorbance, and “η” is implied as “refractive index” of the solvent. To curtail the re-absorption outcome, UV-absorption was kept below 1.0 [73]. Further, we measured the QY using the slope method, where kept UV absorbance value below 0.1.

The synthesized CDs were characterized by the following equipment. Fluorescence spectra were measured via a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA), and UV-V absorption spectra were measured via Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, Largo, FL, USA). The surface charge of CDs was measured using zeta potential & particle size analyzer (Otsuka ELSZ–1000, Tokyo, Japan). Surface functionalities of CDs were determined via Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR APHA II, Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA). Surface composition was assessed by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) using the source type AI K Alpha (Thermo Fisher Scientific, K Alpha+, Waltham, MA, USA). Phase Identification was performed through an X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyzer (Bruker-AXS New D8-Advance). The size and morphological characteristics were evaluated by a field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, JEM-F200, Jeol, Tokyo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of isoprothiolane recovery from spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: Spiked sample analysis was performed on fruit (tomato, grape, and apple) and rice samples. Fresh fruit was bought from a nearby marketplace and used without any further treatment. Juice was extracted from the fresh fruit using a grinder. To remove large particles, the juice was filtered using Whatman filter paper (Grade-292). The 5.0 mL of filter juice was then diluted with 95 mL of DI water. The diluted samples were spiked with different concentrations of isoprothiolane solutions (0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 µM) and then left to settle for 40 min. Thereafter, 12 mL of methanol was added and then vortexed for 10 min to homogenize the solution. Subsequently, 3.2 g of sodium sulfate was added and filtered through a 0.45 μm syringe. The solution was concentrated by evaporation of the solvent. Thereafter, it was analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Rice samples were prepared according to the previous procedure [8]. First, 5.0 g of polished rice was finely ground into powder, and it was then spiked with 1.0 µg/mL isoprothiolane. To stabilize the ground rice, it was extracted in 50% acetone for 24 h. Remained solvents were then completely evaporated. The remaining dry ground rice was then dissolved in 50% (v/v) methanol. Solutions of different concentrations were prepared (0.5 µM, 0.1 µM, and 1.0 µM). The solutions were analyzed via a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The elemental composition and electronic states of the CDs were characterized by XPS analysis. Fig. 2 a–d displays the XPS survey spectrum of the CDs. XPS of the peaks at 284.84 eV, 399.84 eV, and 531.67 eV resemble the elements of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. These results demonstrate that synthesized CDs are mainly comprised of C, O, and N with the elemental percent composition of 71.82%, 19.81%, and 8.37%, respectively. The binding energy peak at 284.84 eV was attributed to the CC bond, and the peak around 286.28 eV was attributed to either the C-N or C-O bond. A peak at 287.75 eV confirms that the presence of carbonyl (CO) bond in CDs. The peak around 399.84 eV reflects the data of (10/20 amino Ns), and the peak around 531.67 eV reveals the C-OH/C-O-C bond of CDs. The XPS data well agree with the FTIR data mentioned above. These analytical data provide confirmatory information about present organic groups in the CDs. Moreover, the elemental composition and content of the probe were presented in tabulated form as Table 2 .

To determine the phase of a crystalline or amorphous structure of CDs, we studied the XRD analyzer (Fig. 2e). The peak at (002) confirms that the synthesized CDs are crystalline. The peaks at 28.37°, 40.62°, and 50.24° correspond to (002), (100), and (102) crystals, where it represents the crystal planes of graphite (sp 2).

The field-emission transmission electron microscopic (FE-TEM) images of CDs are shown in Fig. 3 a and b. These results demonstrated that synthesized CDs are finely dispersed in an aqueous solution with 4.3 ± 0.65 nm diameter and also indicate the d-lattice spacing of 0.19 nm, which is in good conformance with the graphite lattice plane. Further, the XRD spectra and d-lattice spacing obtained from TEM image can be specified that as-synthesized CDs is crystalline in nature. The FE-TEM image of CDs with isoprothiolane reveals that the aggregation occurred in the CDs with the addition of isoprothiolane as shown in Fig. S5.

To ameliorate the potential sensing capacity of CDs for pesticides, effect of pH buffer was scrutinized based on the fluorescence quenching emission spectra of CDs and CDs with isoprothiolane using phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH 7.4) with a range of pH from 2.0 to 12.0. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were recorded at various pHs with and without isoprothiolane. These data revealed that the pH of the solution did not show any effect on the fluorescence sensing of isoprothiolane, and there was no pH variation in the case of bare CDs. Consequently, the isoprothiolane detection was carried out without any pH buffer change (Fig. S6a and S6b).

The decrement of a lifetime from 2.591 ns to 2.521 ns is due to the static quenching mechanism within the concentration range of 0.05 µM–100 µM, where “I0” is the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs. “I” is the fluorescence emission intensities of CDs with the presence of isoprothiolane, “kq” is the bimolecular quenching constant, “τ0 ” is the lifetime of CDs without the presence of isoprothiolane, “Q” is the concentration of pesticide used, and “τ” is the lifetime of CDs with the presence of isoprothiolane. Further, we have added the tabulated form of calculated data as Table 4 .

The propensity of CDs to distinguish isoprothiolane from a group of pesticides (sulfosulfuron, fipronil, clodinafop, imidacloprid, isoproturon, carbendazim, thiamethoxam, hexaconazole, and tricyclazole) was investigated (Fig. 4). It was observed that other pesticides are not susceptible to fluorescence enhancement and quenching. Isoprothiolane was susceptible to quenching even in the presence of other pesticides. Analysis of spectral data revealed that the CDs could distinguish isoprothiolane from other pesticides. To assert that the capacity of CDs as fluorescent sensors for isoprothiolane is not influenced by various inorganic metal ions (cations: Fe2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cr2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, and Ni2+; 1 mM), or various metal anions (SO4 2−, Br−, Cl−, PO4 2−, NO3 −, and S2−; 1 mM), or common potential hindering pesticides (sulfosulfuron, thiamethoxam, tricyclazole, imidacloprid, and fipronil; 1 mM), we investigated all the above potential influencers during the sensing of isoprothiolane by using fruit and rice samples since all the above potential influencers frequently appear in fruit and rice (Table 5 and Fig. S8). It was observed that the fluorescence quenching of CDs was only applied to isoprothiolane. No other material was susceptible to the fluorescence quenching of CDs, revealing the highly biased selectivity of CDs toward isoprothiolane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of isoprothiolane in rice and fruit samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practicability of C. procera-derived CDs for the detection of isoprothiolane, the samples of rice and fruits were spiked with isoprothiolane. Rice and fruits were brought from the nearby store in Surat, Gujarat, India. We extracted the juice and prepared them according to the aforementioned procedures, and then spiked them with isoprothiolane of different concentrations (1.0, 0.5, and 0.1 µM). The amount of isoprothiolane was quantified according to the above-mentioned procedures. The recovery of isoprothiolane was found in the range of 100.77– 101.02% for rice and 95.43–100.68% for fruit samples with the relative standard deviation (RSD) in the range of 0.21–5.72% as shown in Table 5, confirming that our method detects isoprothiolane in rice and fruit with good accuracy and precision.

In summary, we explored a facile green synthetic approach for the fabrication of intense blue fluorescent CDs using C. procera leaves as a carbon source. The unification of CDs displays an intense blue fluorescence emission at 416 nm when it is excited at 340 nm. Analysis of the spectral data revealed that the synthesized probe is rich in organic functional groups. C, O, and N elements with good water dispersibility and size of around 4.3 nm were detected. The CDs acted as a fluorescent probe for the detection of isoprothiolane via a signal turn-off mechanism. Shorten in the lifetime from 2.59 ns to 2.52 ns after the addition of the pesticide provides us the insight view that the quenching is through a static quenching mechanism. The method shows good linearity in the range of 0.05–100 µM with LOD of 11.58 nM (3.36 ppb), which is lower than what previous works have reported. Moreover, the method exhibited several outstanding advantages such as simplicity of the synthesizing procedure, green approach, higher selectivity toward isoprothiolane, and rapid response even with small amounts of isoprothiolane. We successfully applied this method to detect isoprothiolane in rice and fruit samples with good accuracy. As such, this method can be successfully used for the detection of isoprothiolane in real samples. It is a greener approach with rapid response.

Subhadeep Ghosh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Anam Rana Gul: Methodology, Validation, Visualization. Chan Yeong Park: Methodology, Visualization. Ping Xu: Methodology. Seung Hoon Baek: Methodology, Validation. Jigna R. Bhamore: Methodology. Min Woo Kim: Methodology. Min Lee: Writing. Suresh Kumar Kailasa: Resources, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Tae Jung Park: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (NRF-2019R1A2C2084065) and by the Chung-Ang University Research Grants in 2019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106272.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

K. Zhang, M. Tang, J. Zhang, Y. Li, X. Han, S. Pan, X. Kong, M. Li, H. Chen, W. Zhang, Int. J. Environ. Anal. Chem. 96 (2016) 38-49.
